title: Folding and piercing signatures
subtitle: The foundational preparation step every sewn binding requires
excerpt: Every multi-signature binding starts here: folding sheets into sections, collating them, and piercing the sewing stations cleanly. Getting these steps right determines whether the finished book lies flat and whether the thread path holds under use.
sourceNotes: Douglas Cockerell, Bookbinding and the Care of Books (1901), chapters on preparing text block and piercing sewing stations. Internet Archive, archive.org/details/bookbindingandca00cock.

content.text: Before any thread goes through any paper, the text block needs preparing: sheets folded cleanly into
signatures
, collated in order, and pierced at the
sewing stations
that the thread will follow. A signature folded against the
grain direction
will always resist lying flat. A pierced station that wanders off-centre will misalign the text block at the spine. Both are worth getting right at the start.
What you need

items.name: Piercing jig (optional, or a folded strip of scrap card)

content.text: Checking grain direction

Hold a sheet in portrait. Gently bow it top to bottom, then side to side. The direction that bends with less resistance is along the grain. For a book, you want the grain running parallel to the spine, so the pages open without springing back.

A second test: wet a small corner with one fingertip. The sheet will curl: the curl rolls perpendicular to the grain. If both tests give a consistent answer, use it. If they disagree, the paper may be poorly made; choose another.
Folding the sheets
Lay one sheet on a clean flat surface. Bring the two long edges together, checking that the corners align exactly. The fold will run parallel to the grain.

Hold the edges in place with one hand. With the other, run the bone folder firmly along the fold from the centre outward to each end, pressing through any resistance. The fold-line will go slightly translucent where the fibres compact.
Open the sheet and check that the fold is crisp and straight from end to end. Re-fold and re-crease anything that wandered.
Repeat for all sheets in the signature (usually 4 to 8 sheets per signature for most binding styles). Nest them inside each other, spine-aligned, checking that the
foredge
edges are flush across all sheets, with page numbers running in order from the innermost sheet outward.
Marking and piercing sewing stations

Open the signature at the centre fold. On the inner spine, mark the station positions in light pencil. Standard positions for a five-hole binding: 12 mm from the head, 12 mm from the tail, and three evenly spaced stations between.

If using a piercing jig, slide the open signature into the jig and press the stations with the awl through the jig holes. Without a jig: place the open signature flat on the cutting mat and pierce straight down through all layers at each mark.

Hold the awl vertical and press through with a single firm push. A wobbling or angled pierce produces an oval hole that weakens under thread tension. The hole should be barely large enough for the needle to pass; the thread expands the fibres and the stitch tightens naturally.

Make a piercing template from a strip of card cut to the exact height of the book and marked with all station positions. Use it for every subsequent signature so the stations align exactly across the text block.

attrs.intro: Problems that appear during signature preparation.

items.fix: Refold with the bone folder held at a low angle, pressing firmly the full length of the fold. If the grain is wrong, recut the sheets to grain-parallel orientation.
items.cause: Paper folded against the grain or bone-folder crease not pressed firmly enough.
items.symptom: Pages spring open and won't lie flat

items.fix: Make a cardstock piercing template and use it for every signature in the book.
items.cause: Each signature was pierced freehand from new pencil marks rather than from a template.
items.symptom: Sewing stations drift out of alignment across signatures

items.fix: Open the signature completely and work from inside. Keep the awl vertical. Pierce straight down, not at an angle toward the foredge.
items.cause: Awl approaching from the outer cover rather than from inside the open fold, or awl held at an angle.
items.symptom: Awl pushes the signature sheets apart rather than piercing through

attrs.heading: Common problems

content.text: Where this leads
Every sewn binding in this collection begins with this step.
